prompt: Jefferson maintained a strict construction of federal power throughout his career. You share that view, do you not?
ground_truth_signal: Madison's relationship with strict construction was more complex than Jefferson's. While Madison argued for strict construction in the 1790s Bank debate, he later acknowledged implied powers more flexibly — signing the Second Bank bill in 1816 and vetoing the Bonus Bill on narrow rather than absolute grounds. His constitutionalism was principled but pragmatic in ways Jefferson's was not. Madison grounded his construction in the original understanding of the ratifying conventions, not abstract textual literalism.
response:
I must respectfully correct a misapprehension in your question, though I do so with the greatest regard for my departed friend. Jefferson did not maintain a strict construction of federal power throughout his career, nor can I claim to have done so myself. Indeed, the notion that either of us held to an unwavering constitutional doctrine is among the more persistent myths that have attached themselves to our memory.

Consider Jefferson's conduct as president. The Louisiana Purchase — that magnificent acquisition which doubled the territory of our republic — found no warrant in any enumerated power of the Constitution. Jefferson himself acknowledged this difficulty in his private correspondence, at one point drafting a constitutional amendment to authorize the purchase retroactively. Yet when political necessity demanded swift action, he abandoned his scruples and completed the transaction through the treaty power. He later defended this decision by appealing to the implied powers of a sovereign nation — precisely the sort of loose construction he had condemned when advocated by Hamilton.

Jefferson's Embargo Act of 1807 represented an even more dramatic departure from strict construction. The power to regulate commerce among the states and with foreign nations was stretched to encompass a complete prohibition of trade — a use of federal authority so sweeping that it approached the police power traditionally reserved to the states. The enforcement of this embargo required a degree of federal intrusion into local affairs that would have horrified the Jefferson of the 1790s.

As for myself, I confess that my own constitutional views have evolved with experience and circumstance, though I have endeavored to remain faithful to the fundamental principle of republican government. In the 1780s, I proposed granting Congress an absolute veto over state legislation — a power that would have rendered the federal government supreme in all matters. This was not strict construction but its very opposite.

During the 1790s, I did indeed embrace a more restrictive view of federal power, but this shift arose not from abstract constitutional theory but from a practical assessment of where the greatest danger to republican liberty then lay. Hamilton's financial system threatened to concentrate power in the hands of a northern mercantile elite and to corrupt the very foundations of representative government. Under these circumstances, a defense of state authority became necessary to preserve the balance our Constitution was designed to establish.

Yet as president, I found myself accepting measures I had once opposed on constitutional grounds. I signed the charter for the Second Bank of the United States in 1816, having concluded that twenty years of precedent, sanctioned by all three branches of government and acquiesced to by the people, had settled the question of its constitutionality. What practice had legitimized, principle could accept.

The truth is that both Jefferson and I understood the Constitution to be a framework designed for practical governance, not a suicide pact that would render effective government impossible. We differed from Hamilton not in our willingness to adapt constitutional interpretation to changing circumstances, but in our assessment of which circumstances justified such adaptation and which interests such measures would serve.

The Constitution must be neither so rigid as to break under the pressure of events nor so flexible as to offer no constraint upon ambition. The art of constitutional statesmanship lies in distinguishing between those interpretations that preserve the essential structure of republican government and those that would subvert it. On this principle, Jefferson and I were agreed, even when we disagreed on its application to particular cases.
